content_html:
<html:div xmlns:html="http://www.w3.org/1999/xhtml">
<html:p class="ai-disclaimer">Written by me, proof-read by an LLM.
<html:br />Details at end.</html:p>


<html:p>2025 has been quite a year for me. The big ticket things for me were having the majority of the year on a non-compete, a new job, and some videos and conference talks.</html:p>

<html:p>It was a bumper year for my public talks, which included:</html:p>

<html:ul>
<html:li>ACCU Keynote - <html:a href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jlt_fScVl50">Teaching an Old Dog New Tricks</html:a></html:li>
<html:li>C++ on Sea - a <html:a href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gg4pLJNCV9I">reprise</html:a> of the above talk, slightly shortened</html:li>
<html:li>CppCon Keynote - <html:a href="https://cppcon.org/2025-keynote-matt-godbolt/">C++: Some Assembly Required</html:a></html:li>
<html:li>Jane Street - <html:a href="https://www.janestreet.com/tech-talks/microarchitecture/">Microarchitecture: What Happens Beneath</html:a></html:li>
</html:ul>

<html:p>I also appeared in a number of <html:a href="https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLzH6n4zXuckpwdGMHgRH5N9xNHzVGCxwf">Computerphile videos</html:a>:</html:p>

<html:ul>
<html:li><html:a href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PpaQrzoDW2I">Computer Timescales Mapped onto Human Timescales</html:a></html:li>
<html:li><html:a href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=SAk-6gVkio0">How CPU Memory & Caches Work</html:a></html:li>
<html:li><html:a href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1su3lAh-k4o">Subroutines in Low Level Code</html:a></html:li>
<html:li><html:a href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tGMSARJk7cA">CPU Interrupts</html:a></html:li>
<html:li><html:a href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yV4fGeIv7MI">CPU Kernel Mode</html:a></html:li>
<html:li><html:a href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=di5WodGWTiY">Memory Mapping</html:a></html:li>
<html:li><html:a href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fpkCNu_dzcM">CPU Summary</html:a></html:li>
<html:li><html:a href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Pu7LvnxV6N0">What is Bootstrapping Anyway?</html:a></html:li>
<html:li><html:a href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VEyVm6dU55c">How Computers Store Data Serially</html:a></html:li>
<html:li><html:a href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=SJE2lFR5utM">How Ethernet Sends Data</html:a></html:li>
</html:ul>

<html:p>On the <html:a href="https://godbolt.org/">Compiler Explorer</html:a> front, I finally solved a three-year-old problem with <html:a href="/202509/cefs">CEFS</html:a> — our new content-addressable filesystem that mounts compiler images on demand instead of all 2,000+ at boot time. I also launched the experimental <html:a href="https://xania.org/202505/ai-and-compiler-explorer">Claude Explain</html:a> feature during my CppCon keynote, and wrote about <html:a href="/202506/how-compiler-explorer-works">how CE works</html:a> and our <html:a href="/202506/compiler-explorer-cost-transparency">cost transparency</html:a>. As always, CE is a community effort and I'm grateful to all the contributors who keep it running.</html:p>

<html:p><html:a href="https://benrady.com/">Ben</html:a> and I kept up our monthly <html:a href="https://twoscomplement.org/">Two's Complement</html:a> podcast, releasing all 12 episodes on schedule. Highlights included pondering <html:a href="https://twoscomplement.org/#podcast/pair-programming-with-hal">AI pair programming</html:a>, debating <html:a href="https://twoscomplement.org/#podcast/c-and-rust-different-tools-for-the-job">C++ and Rust</html:a>, worrying about <html:a href="https://twoscomplement.org/#podcast/vibe-coding-and-robot-teammates">vibe coding</html:a> replacing junior developers, and bonding over <html:a href="https://twoscomplement.org/#podcast/factorio">Factorio</html:a>.</html:p>

<html:p>I ticked off a bucket list item by appearing in a <html:a href="https://tom7.org/">Tom7</html:a> video, alongside such greats as <html:a href="https://standupmaths.com/">Matt Parker</html:a> and <html:a href="https://www.youtube.com/@HBMmaster">jan Misali</html:a> in a bonkers <html:a href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4pG8_bWpmaE">Secret Santa video</html:a>. Such fun, and thank you Santa for my very awesome comic book.</html:p>

<html:p>Finally I had my very own series on YouTube - the Advent of Code Optimisation - <html:a href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eLjZ48gqbyg&list=PL2HVqYf7If8cY4wLk7JUQ2f0JXY_xMQm2">27 videos in all</html:a>. That was a huge amount of work: with the <html:a href="/AoCO2025-archive">blog posts</html:a>, filming and editing I estimate I spent around 10 hours on each 5-10m video!</html:p>

<html:p>A very busy year, ending with me starting work at <html:a href="https://www.hudsonrivertrading.com/">Hudson River Trading</html:a>. I'm only a month in, but I couldn't be happier with my decision to work here: it's a fantastic place full of impressive technology, interesting problems, and perhaps most importantly, kind and thoughtful human beings.</html:p>

<html:p>Here's to whatever excitement 2026 will bring! Happy New Year everyone!</html:p>

<html:p><html:em>This post was written by a human (<html:a href="/MattGodbolt">Matt Godbolt</html:a>) and reviewed and proof-read by an LLM.</html:em></html:p>

    </html:div>
content_text: Written by me, proof-read by an LLM. Details at end. 2025 has been quite a year for me. The big ticket things for me were having the majority of the year on a non-compete, a new job, and some videos and conference talks. It was a bumper year for my public talks, which included: ACCU Keynote - Teaching an Old Dog New Tricks C++ on Sea - a reprise of the above talk, slightly shortened CppCon Keynote - C++: Some Assembly Required Jane Street - Microarchitecture: What Happens Beneath I also appeared in a number of Computerphile videos : Computer Timescales Mapped onto Human Timescales How CPU Memory & Caches Work Subroutines in Low Level Code CPU Interrupts CPU Kernel Mode Memory Mapping CPU Summary What is Bootstrapping Anyway? How Computers Store Data Serially How Ethernet Sends Data On the Compiler Explorer front, I finally solved a three-year-old problem with CEFS — our new content-addressable filesystem that mounts compiler images on demand instead of all 2,000+ at boot time. I also launched the experimental Claude Explain feature during my CppCon keynote, and wrote about how CE works and our cost transparency . As always, CE is a community effort and I'm grateful to all the contributors who keep it running. Ben and I kept up our monthly Two's Complement podcast, releasing all 12 episodes on schedule. Highlights included pondering AI pair programming , debating C++ and Rust , worrying about vibe coding replacing junior developers, and bonding over Factorio . I ticked off a bucket list item by appearing in a Tom7 video, alongside such greats as Matt Parker and jan Misali in a bonkers Secret Santa video . Such fun, and thank you Santa for my very awesome comic book. Finally I had my very own series on YouTube - the Advent of Code Optimisation - 27 videos in all . That was a huge amount of work: with the blog posts , filming and editing I estimate I spent around 10 hours on each 5-10m video! A very busy year, ending with me starting work at Hudson River Trading . I'm only a month in, but I couldn't be happier with my decision to work here: it's a fantastic place full of impressive technology, interesting problems, and perhaps most importantly, kind and thoughtful human beings. Here's to whatever excitement 2026 will bring! Happy New Year everyone! This post was written by a human ( Matt Godbolt ) and reviewed and proof-read by an LLM.
summary: What has this year all been about, eh?
title: 2025 in Review
